question:
The amount of nitrogen (in kilotonnes per annum) entering UK coastal waters via fresh water inputs (i.e. from rivers) is compared to that entering directly into saline waters from point sources (e.g. pipes):
\begin{tabular}{|l|l|l|l|l|l|l|}
\hline & & \multicolumn{5}{|l|}{Region} \\
\hline & & Atlantic & North Sea (East coast) & North Sea (Channel) & Celtic Sea & Irish Sea \\
\hline Route & Rivers & 28 & 93 & 17 & 30 & 42 \\
\hline & Direct & 10 & 77 & 12 & 11 & 6 \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
(i) Perform a two-way ANOVA with single observations in each cell (assume there is no interaction between input route and region) to decide whether there is a difference in the amount of nitrogen entering coastal waters: (a) via the two routes; and (b) between regions. Note that the amount of nitrogen entering coastal waters via the two routes could be compared using a paired $t$ test-and yield the same result-but this would not provide information on variation between regions.

\begin{tabular}{|l|l|l|l|l|l|}
\hline & df & SS & MS & F & P \\
\hline Route & & & & & \\
\hline Region & & & & & \\
\hline Remainder & & & & & \\
\hline Total & & & & & \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
(ii) Using multiple comparison tests where appropriate, what can you conclude from these results?
answer:
(i)

\begin{tabular}{|l|l|l|l|l|l|}
\hline & df & SS & MS & $F$ & $P$ \\
\hline Route & 1 & 883.600 & 883.600 & 14.286 & 0.0194 \\
\hline Region & 4 & 6957.400 & 1739.350 & 28.122 & 0.0035 \\
\hline Remainder & 4 & 247.000 & 61.850 & & \\
\hline Total & 9 & 8088.000 & & & \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
(ii) River input is significantly higher than direct input River $=42$; Direct $=23.2$ ), and there is a significant difference among regions. Tukey's multiple comparisons, reveal that the North Sea East Coast has significantly higher nitrogen input than all the other regions ( $P<0.05$ ). None of the other regions differ from each other: North Sea East Coast $>$ (Irish Sea, Celtic Sea, Atlantic, North Sea Channel)
